draft.introduction: John 6:53–58 is the most physically shocking passage in the New Testament. Jesus tells his audience that unless they eat his flesh and drink his blood, they have no life in them. The Greek is unsparing: the verb trogon (τρώγων) means not merely “to eat” but “to gnaw, to munch”—the word used for animals feeding. Many of his disciples left him over these words. The passage is deliberately offensive because the claim it makes is deliberately extreme: the divine enters the human not through the mind but through the mouth, not through contemplation but through consumption. The Church Fathers read this passage as the foundation of Eucharistic theology—the bread and wine of the Lord’s Supper are the flesh and blood of Christ. But the theosis tradition heard something more: that the one who consumes Christ’s body is transformed by what is consumed. As Irenaeus put it, the Eucharist is the medicine of immortality. The text is from Codex Sinaiticus.

translation.text: So Jesus said to them, “Truly, truly, I say to you: unless you eat the flesh of the Son of Man and drink his blood, you have no life in you. Whoever feeds on (trogon, τρώγων) my flesh and drinks my blood has eternal life, and I will raise him up on the last day.”
translation.original_text: εἶπεν οὖν αὐτοῖς ὁ Ιησοῦς · ἀμὴν ἀμὴν λέγω ὑμῖν · ἐὰν μὴ φάγητε τὴν σάρκα τοῦ υἱοῦ τοῦ ἀνθρώπου καὶ πίητε αὐτοῦ τὸ αἷμα · οὐκ ἔχετε ζωὴν ἐν ἑαυτοῖς · ὁ τρώγων μου τὴν σάρκα καὶ πίνων μου τὸ αἷμα ἔχει ζωὴν αἰώνιον ·

translation.text: “For my flesh is true food, and my blood is true drink. Whoever feeds on my flesh and drinks my blood abides in me, and I in him.”
translation.original_text: ἡ γὰρ σάρξ μου ἀληθής ἐστιν βρῶσις · καὶ τὸ αἷμά μου ἀληθής ἐστιν πόσις · ὁ τρώγων μου τὴν σάρκα καὶ πίνων μου τὸ αἷμα ἐν ἐμοὶ μένει κἀγὼ ἐν αὐτῷ ·

translation.text: “As the living Father sent me, and I live because of the Father, so whoever feeds on me will also live because of me. This is the bread that came down from heaven, not like the bread the fathers ate, and died. Whoever feeds on this bread will live forever.”
translation.original_text: καθὼς ἀπέστειλέν με ὁ ζῶν πατὴρ κἀγὼ ζῶ διὰ τὸν πατέρα · καὶ ὁ τρώγων με κἀκεῖνος ζήσει δι’ ἐμέ · οὗτός ἐστιν ὁ ἄρτος ὁ ἐξ οὐρανοῦ καταβάς ·

translator_notes.note: The shift from phagein (φαγεῖν, “to eat”) in verse 53 to trogon (τρώγων, “to gnaw, to chew”) in verse 54 is deliberate and shocking. The first verb is neutral; the second is visceral. Jesus is not softening his language as the crowd grows uncomfortable—he is intensifying it. The insistence on physical consumption was the primary text the Church used against any purely symbolic reading of the Eucharist.

The mutual indwelling formula—“abides in me, and I in him”—is the Johannine expression of what later theology would call perichoresis (mutual interpenetration). It is the same logic as Gospel of Thomas Saying 108: the consumer and the consumed become one. Cyril of Alexandria used this passage to argue that the Eucharist effects a physical union between Christ and the believer, making the human body a vessel of divine life. This is theosis through the stomach.
